revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''官位令'''（かんいりょう）は、[[律令|令]]の篇目の1つ。[[大宝律令|大宝令]]・[[養老律令|養老令]]ではともに第1番…」
revision.text:
'''官位令'''（かんいりょう）は、[[律令|令]]の篇目の1つ。[[大宝律令|大宝令]]・[[養老律令|養老令]]ではともに第1番目に位置しており、全19条からなる。
== 概要 == 官職の位階への対応を定めたもので、親王の一品から四品までの4階、諸王・諸臣の[[正一位]]から[[少初位下]]までの30階について、位階に相応する[[四等官]]・[[品官]]・才伎[[長上]]など内長上の官職を列記している。唐令の官品令に相当するものであるが、官品を正一品から従九品下まで品階に分け、その尊卑を定める基準であり、それ自体が官人に与えられるものではなかった。日本の場合は、[[位階]]が各官人に与えられるものであり、官職につくには一定の位階を持たなければならないという、日本独自の[[官位相当制]]の原則があり、唐制に比して、より身分制的な性格を持っている。ほかにも軍功により与えられる[[勲位]]の規定もあり、十二等まであり、朝参行列などの際の勲位と位階の対応関係が示されていた。
== 参考文献 == * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p263、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p227、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『日本史史料［1］古代』編者：歴史学研究会、2005年
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]]
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんいりよう}} [[Category:日本の令]]

contributor.username: ja>壁掛成蛸は三考する葦である

== 概要 == 官職の位階への対応を定めたもので、親王の一品から四品までの4階、諸王・諸臣の[[正一位]]から[[少初位下]]までの30階について、位階に相応する[[四等官]]・[[品官]]・才伎[[長上]]など内長上の官職を列記している。唐令の官品令に相当するものであるが、官品を正一品から従九品下まで品階に分け、その尊卑を定める基準であり、それ自体が官人に与えられるものではなかった。日本の場合は、[[位階]]が各官人に与えられるものであり、官職につくには一定の位階を持たなければならないという、日本独自の[[官位相当制]]の原則があり、唐制に比して、より身分制的な性格を持っている。ほかにも軍功により与えられる[[勲位]]の規定もあり、十二等まであり、朝参行列などの際の勲位と位階の対応関係が示されていた。
== 参考文献 == * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p227、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p263、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『日本史史料［1］古代』編者：歴史学研究会、2005年

revision.text:
'''官位令'''（かんいりょう）は、[[律令|令]]の篇目の1つ。[[大宝律令|大宝令]]・[[養老律令|養老令]]ではともに第1番目に位置しており、全19条からなると推測されている<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=井上光貞の業績と『令集解』研究 |year=2019 |publisher=[[明治大学日本古代学研究所]] |pages=35-41 |series=日本古代学研究の世界的拠点形成 |author=[[吉村武彦]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=坂上康俊|year=1998|title=大宝令復原考証三題|journal=[[史淵]]|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher=九州大学文学部}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 官職の位階への対応を定めたもので、親王の一品から四品までの4階、諸王・諸臣の[[正一位]]から[[少初位下]]までの30階について、位階に相応する[[四等官]]・[[品官]]・才伎[[長上]]など内長上の官職を列記している。唐令の官品令に相当するものであるが、官品を正一品から従九品下まで品階に分け、その尊卑を定める基準であり、それ自体が官人に与えられるものではなかった。日本の場合は、[[位階]]が各官人に与えられるものであり、官職につくには一定の位階を持たなければならないという、日本独自の[[官位相当制]]の原則があり、唐制に比して、より身分制的な性格を持っている。ほかにも軍功により与えられる[[勲位]]の規定もあり、十二等まであり、朝参行列などの際の勲位と位階の対応関係が示されていた。
== 脚注・参考文献 == === 脚注 === {{reflist}}
=== 参考文献 === * 『角川第二版日本史辞典』p227、[[高柳光寿]]・[[竹内理三]]：編、[[角川書店]]、1966 * 『岩波日本史辞典』p263、監修：[[永原慶二]]、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『日本史史料［1］古代』編者：歴史学研究会、2005年
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[令義解]] * [[令集解]]
{{japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんいりよう}} [[Category:日本の令]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:官位令]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q97341948]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|qq=|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|qq=|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|qq=|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|qq=|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|qq=|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|qq=|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|qq=|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|qq=|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:官位令]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97341948]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] codes. In both the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]], it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]] in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} codes. In both the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}, it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system}} in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

In both the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}, it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. == Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system}} in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} codes. In both the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}, it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=honkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=supervisory roles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system}} in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system|1=List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles}} in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.

'''Kan'iryō''' (官位令) is one of the chapters of the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} codes. In both the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}, it occupies the first position and is believed to consist of 19 articles.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=The Achievements of Inoue Mitsusada and Research on "Ryōshūge" |year=2019 |publisher={{ill|Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja|明治大学日本古代学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji University Institute of Ancient Japanese Studies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |pages=35-41 |series=Formation of a Global Hub for Research on Ancient Japanese Studies |author={{ill|Yoshimura Takehiko|ja|吉村武彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimura Takehiko|1=Takehiko Yoshimura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413279}} |url=https://www.meiji.ac.jp/research/promote/strategic/6t5h7p00000rtjvy-att/a1559088837624.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|author=Sakagami Yasutoshi|year=1998|title=Three Studies on the Restoration of the Taihō Code |url=https://api.lib.kyushu-u.ac.jp/opac_download_md/1867921/p001.pdf.pdf|journal={{ill|Shien|ja|史淵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shien|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|volume=135|pages=1-17|publisher={{ill|Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja|九州大学文学部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu University Faculty of Letters|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九州大学大学院人文科学研究院・大学院人文科学府・文学部|1=Q56346425|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56346425}}}}</ref>.

== Overview ==

The Kan'iryō stipulates the correspondence between {{ill|government posts|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/government posts|1=East Asian government position|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452125}} and {{ill|court Ranks|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Court Ranks in Japan|WD=Q99196082}}. It defines four Ranks for princes, from {{ill|First Rank|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/First Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王}} to {{ill|Fourth Rank|ja|四品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}}, and 30 Ranks for other royal family members and officials, from {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank Lower|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank Lower|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}. These Ranks are linked to specific {{ill|Four-Tier Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}, {{ill|honorary positions|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honorary positions|1=Hinkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418352}}, and {{ill|supervisory roles|ja|長上|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/supervisory roles|1=Q11651393|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651393}}.

The Kan'iryō corresponds to the {{ill|Tang Code's official Rank system|ja|唐令の官品令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tang Code's official Rank system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} but differs significantly in its application. In Tang China, Ranks were divided into official grades (from {{ill|Zhengyi Pin|ja|正一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengyi Pin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 品位 (位階)|1=Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q240268}} to {{ill|Congjiu Pinxia|ja|従九品下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Congjiu Pinxia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as standards of hierarchical distinction and were not necessarily bestowed on officials. In contrast, under the Japanese system, court Ranks were individually conferred, and holding a certain Rank was a prerequisite for appointment to government posts, reflecting the unique {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system|1=List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles}} in Japan. This system had a more rigid class structure than its Chinese counterpart.

Additionally, the Kan'iryō regulated {{ill|merit Ranks|ja|勲位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/merit Ranks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勲等|1=Japanese honors system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17990718}}, which were granted based on military achievements. There were twelve levels of merit Ranks, and the code detailed their correspondence to court Ranks for use in ceremonial settings, such as processions.
